comment_content:
Content is great. We learnt a lot of interesting topics surrounding the field of AI, including Naive Bayes, K-nearest neighbours (tho I heard that in the coming semesters, linear regression might replace KNN in the syllabus), K-means clustering, and artificial neurons. Some of the more advanced topics include image processing and convolutional neural networks. Lastly, we learnt minimax and alpha-beta pruning, which are search algorithms for games.

The lecture slides are designed very well.
comment_teaching:
The instructor teaches VERY WELL. She always teaches in a way that makes it easy to understand complex concepts. She also answers students' questions very patiently. Oh, and she always gives us 5 minutes break in the middle of the lecture, which really helps me when I need some more time to understand the concepts or when I need to ask her questions.

HOWEVER, the TAs are BAD. Basically, the main purpose of going to the labs is just to take attendance. The lab's teaching TA's voice is basically as low as if it is only listenable to the people who are no more than 0.5 m away from him. Most of the time, we can't even notice that he started the teaching because, yeah.

Also, the assignments are so problematic. I will explain more under the "WORKLOAD" part.
comment_workload:
Assignments and labs are SO PROBLEMATIC.

Many bugs in their skeleton, many unclear instructions, many untaught things which appear in the assignments. They really expect us to deep-learn the unclear documentation on some library functions that are OUT-OF-THE-TEACHING-SYLLABUS.

Half of the last programming assignment was about the preprocessing of texts. Well, you might think --- yeah, that looks important in AI. BUT, if we have just 1 out of 30000 results that is [NOT WRONG, but a little DIFFERENT from their result], then good game. **IT IS NOT ABOUT RIGHT OR WRONG ---- IT IS ABOUT GUESSING THE TA'S MODEL ANSWER'S THINKING LOGIC.** The instructions are not clear enough that the preprocessing can be done in MANY DIFFERENT WAYS, in which you need to try like a thousand times to get it done in the way the TA wants you to do.
comment_grading:
I got an A+, but at what cost?

Each of the 2 programming assignments, which are DESIGNED BADLY (I'll explain more under "WORKLOAD"), contributes to 15% of the course grade. That means 30% goes to programming assignments.

Midterm and Final are like 20% and 40%, respectively. Labs are 10%. Labs require attendance, and also the 4 best scores out of 5 lab exercises are counted.

I got like 98 marks in the midterm (which is quite a lot higher than the mean). But what does that mean? 20%. Only. The midterm is like treating us as if our brains were AI. A lot of calculations that can make your brain explode. The time limit was short as well.

In comparison, failing just ONE test case in a programming assignment costs you 1% deduction in the whole course grade. Which is equivalent to losing 5 marks in a midterm exam. What does that mean? A very complicated calculation that would drive you crazy might earn you 2 marks in the midterm. Yeah.

comment_grading:
Desmond promises 25% A range so its extremely good grading. But bare in mind that people taking this course has an extended major in AI. So be prepared as they are generally more competitive than other students. Anyway, these are my marks: (LQ/Median/ Mean/UQ/Max)
Lab  full marks
PA1 16.5/16.5 (15/15.95/15.54/16.5/16.5)
PA2 14.36/15 (8.54/11.25/10.1/12.32/15)
Midterm 84.7/100 (49.35/60.25/58.93/70.25/92.7)
Final 90.75/100 (54.33/69/64.07/76.45/94.5)
Course Total 92.6/100 (65.65/75.5/71.87/81.56/95.92)
Grade A+

I would like to share some of my thoughts on how you could excel in this course. (Just personal opinion)
Firstly, as mentioned above, you should try your best to learn vectorization. You have to use it in labs, pas and also exams. I know that it is extremely frustrating to learn it as a beginner in coding, but this skill is extremely important in this course. I also struggled a lot in learning it and eventually I became good enough to be able to figure out labs and pas on my own. (I know the logic to doing it but sometimes have minor bugs in my code. Background: COMP student who have taken COMP2011 and 2012 already)

Exams: Spend time to really understand the concepts and memorize small details in slides. The Exams are pretty concept oriented and some questions are just math problems. I still remember we have to prove mathematically why a perceptron with sigma activation function cannot deal with linearly inseparable data points. If you are not good at maths, um... good luck.
For me, I think I understand each topic thoroughly, so I pretty much just write whatever I think on the exam paper. And most of them turn out to be correct.
Also, there's always one coding question taking up around 14-16 marks of the whole paper. My advice would be to NEVER give up. Even if you do not know how to do it at all, scribble something random. If you left it blank, the tas can't give you any marks. I remember writing a completely wrong solution on my coding question on final exam, and was expecting 2/6 at most and probably 1/6. But the TA gave me 4/6, which is ridiculous. I think because most people are leaving it blank, they are extremely lenient in marking those questions. So NEVER, NEVER, NEVER leave those questions blank.

comment_grading:
I did bad in midterm. I misunderstood the focus of the exam. I put more effort and did better in final.
Now, in my point of view:
1. There is a Python programming problem, and a hard subproblem is worth 6-7 points, so it is important to practice using broadcasting proficiently to deal with it.
2. Generally speaking, pay attention to python coding, such as building models and performing operations like reshaping, to avoid getting stuck when using these lines.
3. Besides the above, I think one can treat this course as a Liberal arts minor course, like 史地政 courses in high school. Recite the characteristics of different topics, familiarize yourself with terms and vocabulary to express yourself clearly.

下面我将吐槽一下PA2。
public test case测的时候什么问题都没有，hidden test case全是问题。我记得官方说hidden test case的设置目的是防止hard code，那么为什么难度的差异这么大呢，为什么public test这么不反映问题呢？这造成，我（我猜也包括一些同学，但此处不敢代表所有人）对于hidden test的难度一无所知，不知道努力/优化到什么程度才算ok。比如，test case11对efficiency的要求很模糊，先说了可以不vectorize，又说推荐优化，结果却是不vectorize就会挂。accuracy 75%同样如此。提交时无法知道模型质量，只能出分后抓瞎。ps，判断保留哪个视频这种事情，又没有明确进行提示，每个人的理解各不相同，（尝试起来又很耗时，所以我不想尝试所有可能，）让这种个人理解而非学习/代码能力的事情影响作业成绩，是否区分得有些草率了？
之前不知道一个PA能把人创到这种程度。这下领教了。

comment_content:
Advanced Python knowledge(should replace the goofy turtle in 1021), Naive Bayes, K-Nearest Neighbors, K-Means Clustering, Perceptron, Multilayer Perceptron, Image processing, Convolutional Neural network, Minimax and Alpha Beta pruning, Ai Ethics.
This is not a purely coding course (Python is used btw). It’s more about applying ideas through coding. The contents are well-designed to provide a deeper understanding of how algorithms in Al work. If you plan to take 4211 in the future, this course will definitely help you build a solid foundation. According to the feedback of our seniors, the concepts taught in this course have proven to be valuable during internships as well.

comment_workload: To be honest, the labs and PAs in this semester were not easy to manage. Basically, each assignment primarily focused on applying vectorization, i.e. NumPy functions to handle arrays. It’s more about math than coding! I feel like there isn’t much connection between lecture materials and homeworks. Is it just me? When doing Labs and PAs, I had to search online and ask AI frequently for some Numpy or other AI methods that I was not sure about. Lab 2, was designed to be extremely hard as a convention.(Desmond said he doesn't want the hard topics of different courses to overlap, so he made the earliest graded lab to be the hardest) The subsequent labs were easier than lab 2 but still not that easy. For example, it took a lot of time to run the model in lab 8, I even needed to create another google account for free GPU. For PA 1, We were required to write around 25 functions for vectorization to implement KNN and K-means. Because of the difficulty, the teaching team extended the deadline and gave bonus to students submitting before the original deadline.(10% on PA1 score that can be used to offset deduction on PA2) PA 2(designed by ‘notorious’ Brian, Jesus the 2 lowest-scoring PA I did this semester were all made by you, another one being in 2012, thank you :))) is based on the scenario of autopilot, focusing on image processing and CNN. We still had to write some functions for vectorization. Moreover, we needed to train our own model to detect the directions, which was very time consuming. The worst part, however, was that the provided test cases were almost useless, so we had to manually check everything ourselves.

comment_content:
Probably the best COMP course in terms of content. Useful, practical, and interesting. In a nutshell, it's mostly introduction to AI, ML and image processing without using advanced math, which is perfect for someone like me who knows nothing about before. And I think taking this course alone is already enough for you to intern for some entry level data science stuff.

That said, this course is definitely not a easy course. The most difficult part for me is programming with Numpy (yes, the first chapter), you need to have good multi-dimensional thinking skills to solve the programming problems. I did well in 1021 with Turtle, but here I feel like an idiot with Numpy :( Hope the new COMP1023 will help people prepare more for it.

comment_workload:
The workload is not light, but A here cuz I don't wanna break my straight A rating for the course :)

Lab:
I spent the most time on Lab 2 (the first graded lab) cuz it requires only Numpy programming. Other labs (except Lab 9 Minimax) allow other libraries so are more simple.

PA:
That's where the pain comes from. PA1 and PA2 are two different types of pain.

PA1: You have to complete all tasks in Numpy, and no for loops is allowed for most tasks. I used several blank sheets just to visualize 3D array operations. Now if you see people drawing random squares and cubes on paper in LC, you could guess what they're doing.

PA2: To get full marks your model has to get >=75% accuracy. Imagine spending 20 minutes to train your model and it only got a 60% accuracy, then you repeat this process again and again for 50 times but it's still below 75%. (Or worse, it got a 99% accuracy and obviously it’s overfitting.) As Google Colab limits daily GPU use, I've opened several new accounts just for working on this PA.
Finally I still couldn't get 75% accuracy, but fortunately I still got full marks for PA thanks to early submission bonus in PA1.

comment_content:
In short, I have learn a lot initiative idea of various AI algorithm and coding method where mainly for classification and only high school level math are required to understand most of the things in this course.
---
You will first learn some advance python in first two-three weeks since the basic python in COMP1021 is not enough to help us write AI code. Where here will introduce the mindset of vectorize (Writing code without using for loop), which is very important technique for your coding later in this course/other AI course.*

Then you will learn various of AI algorithm: (Arrange in taught order)
Naïve Bayes
K-Nearest-Neighbors Classifier
K-Means Clustering
Artificial Neural Network - Perceptron
Artificial Neural Network - Multilayer Perceptron
Digital Image Processing Fundamentals
Convolutional Neural Network
Minimax and Alpha-Beta Pruning
Introduction to Reinforcement Learning (Self-Study)^

Where most of the AI algorithm taught here is classification algorithm, which is rarely seen because other foundation course are most likely focus on regression algorithm.

And course instructor will try to tell you the initiative idea of how the algorithm come, when and why we use the algorithm, instead of just reading notes and letting you recite the algorithm for no reason.

At the end of this course, you will learn some AI ethics where I treat it as common sense.

If you want to know more about the algorithm (e.g. how to proof for the error function). Course instructor also provide addition material for you to read, which won't cover in exam.

* It might be remove from the syllabus from future offer semester and these part of content will be move on to COMP1023 (New course that start offer in 202526 Fall)

^ As *, it might be cover in lesson when the remove of advance python in future offer semester.

comment_content:
Check other reviews for table of content.
Overall I would say the course is well designed to give you a taste of some fundamental AI. My reason for taking this course was just to see what it's all about and get a high level idea which the course did quite well.
That being said, bear in mind that this course will cover lots of topics but can't go too deep. Hence, there will be lots of black box. For example you'll just be told to use some specific activation functions and some specific number of layers without explanation.
comment_teaching: I mean it's Desmond, do I need to say anything?
comment_workload:
Overall the workload is medium, or at least it's as much as should be expected from a 2xxx comp course.
The labs are doable within a few hours and the PAs are doable within a day. One thing to note is that the difficulty of this course decreases over time (since we later use libraries :p). So PA1 is probably as hard as this course gets (which is not too hard).
Another thing is that desmond takes extra classes every other week since he goes very slow.
The concepts are not really hard to internalize, but there are many concepts.
Having said that, Desmond gives a lot of time for everything (so much that I forgot to do one of the labs o.O) so you can usually do coursework whenever you have time.
comment_grading:
For a non honours comp course, the grading is as good as it gets. Desmond promises around 25% A range.
Firstly, there's lab attendance marks which I dont like at all (even tho I still skipped because the weightage is not too much)
You can check the weightages in other reviews. Pretty much everyone gets full on labs and nearly full on PAs so let me just comment on the exams.
The mid term was garbage. Just basic arithmetic but really fast. I believe it was made by one of the TAs. But the final was made by desmond and it was really good. There were easy questions for everyone to grab and some harder conceptual questions to made a good curve. Make sure you conceptually understand the high level ideas to perform good.

comment_teaching:
Her teaching was decent. She was eager to answer students' questions.

TAs were decently active in piazza, tho they were quite inactive when we were doing Lab 6, Lab 8 and PA 2 (I say this because I answered quite a lot of questions during that time)
comment_workload:
The workload was what I expected as a 3-credit course. Lab should take at most 1 day to complete, and programming assignments should take at most 1 week. (Note that I usually do my homework very slowly. That's why I took a day to do simple hw. You can easily finish it faster than me.)

Mid-term Exam and Final Exam were very difficult. You can understand why no one got >90.
Mid-term exam was a challenge in terms of speed. You need to do tasks that you have learnt pretty quickly.
Expect:
One-line Python Programming
Burning-eye table for Naive Bayes

Final exam was a challenge in terms of difficulty. You need to not only deal with already complicated Python programming, but also understand stuff that you have never learnt before.
Expect:
Python Programming for topics that you have learnt
New parameters for functions involving neural network
A lot of explanations

comment_content:
You have near zero AI knowledge (like me)? well, you're at the right place, this course is for you

The course title "Exploring Artificial Intelligence" really lives up to its name, here you'll be exposed to (at introductory level) a wide variety of AI/ML techniques and their applications in various fields e.g. predicting your grade, find optimal move in some games, classifying images

Each topic is introduced with rich examples, diagrams and graphics, which is highly beginner friendly. You also have hands-on opportunity to implement AL algorithms in python either from scratch or with library like keras.

mid term: T/F, python, naive bayes, knn , kmeans, Model Evaluation, D-fold Cross Validation , MLP

final: T/F, python, knn, kmeans, MLP, Image Processing, cnn , Minimax, Ethics
comment_teaching:
Desmond is very supportive, in class he always try to give satisfactory answers to questions from students, you can sometimes find him chatting with students after/outside class too.

Desmond is also flexible when it comes to teaching, when he observe many students are confused with a topic, he may send us email for additional explanation or run a few more examples.

The TAs also often provide helpful answers. The lab materials are well designed, they're fun and provide opportunities for you to apply what you have learned in lecture.
comment_workload:
Even I'm total beginner in AI, I'm still able to complete the labs and PA in a few hours so the workload is reasonable.

Before exam I reviewed slides/lecture/labs. I didn't do past paper in mid term, but did in final because Desmond explicitly told us to. Turns out it was quite useful because I became more familiar with exam format and what they expect us to do.
comment_grading:
I didn't get full mark in PA and labs. Only around 5 and 10 marks above UQ in mid term and final respectively. To my great surprise the letter grade is A, very lenient !

(by the way, physical attendance required for labs this sem)

One thing to note is that this course is calculation focused, so do more dry run on examples when you're doing revision e.g. given data, manually do prediction with naive bayes, manually do convolution ... also make sure you can type very fast with your calculator (which you'll bring to exam)

comment_content:
▷ Introduction to & History of AI
▷ Python Fundamentals for AI
    • Python basics (COMP1021 + List comprehension, set?, parallel iteration, class)
    • Libraries used in AI
    • ***NumPy*** nD array reshaping and broadcasting can be confusing but are very important to fully understand!
▷ Naive Bayes Classifier
▷ K-Nearest Neighbours KNN
▷ K-Means Clustering KMC
▷ Artificial Neural Network - Perceptron
▷ Artificial Neural Network - Multilayer Perceptron MLP
▷ Digital Image Processing Fundamentals
▷ Convolutional Neural Network CNN
▷ Minimax & Alpha-Beta Pruning
    • AI in perfect information, zero-sum, deterministic games like tick-tac-toe
▷ Ethics of AI
▷ Reinforcement Learning (Additional, optional lecture after exam)

Overall very practical and interesting content, and can be useful for capstone projects. Mostly on machine learning (a subset of AI), only minimax & alpha-beta pruning is classic AI.

A little overlap with COMP1942 (a data mining course) on Naive Bayes, KNN, and K-means clustering, but since this course does coding, I'd say 2211 is more practical, while 1942 is more in-depth with the theory and manual execution of the algorithm.
comment_teaching:
Interesting teaching by Desmond as expected, explaining the content very well (even sometimes repeating in English, Cantonese, AND Mandarine). Quite deserving to be praised by many COMP students. Some may find the pacing slow, but I feel like it is about right to understand and follow along.

Quick support from TAs on Piazza as well.
comment_workload:
5 graded labs (~bi-weekly) + 2 PAs

= Time & Braincells spent =
Can be time-consuming and even frustrating to figure out. Lab 2, 4, and PA 1 were the most painful since we manually implement the algorithm with only NumPy while being unfamiliar with it. Later on, we can use libraries like SciKitLearn and Keras, BUT fine-tuning the training model hyperparameters will take the majority of the time instead of coding.

= The code =
The programs were saved in Jupyter Notebooks (.ipynb) and executed on Google Colab. No setup is required for GColab but for later labs where you need to train a model, you need to be wary of the limited GPU usage quota. If you have a decent PC, I highly recommend setting up your local Anaconda environment and VSCode so that you can train on your own GPU w/ Tensorflow GPU (which in my Lab 8 is 4s/epoch on RTX3060 compared to 11s/epoch on GColab's Nvidea T4). Yes, it will be quite a hassle, but you will thank it for speeding up training by a margin and allowing you to test more model configurations.
▷ Anaconda Navigator: https://docs.anaconda.com/free/navigator/index.html
(Note that Tensorflow GPU needs to specifically be installed through your environment's terminal and then "pip -install tensorflow-gpu". It might be different for you, but that's how I got mine working)

= ZINC =
Be VERY CAREFUL in checking your code! ZINC will NOT generate a grading report when you submit so you won't know if your code is bugged or wrong. Batch grading in 2211 has crashed ZINC multiple times this semester :P.

= Labs (G for graded) =
1. NumPy
2. Naive Bayes Classifier (G)
3. KNN
4. KMC (G)
5. Simple Perceptron
6. MLP (G)
7. Digital Image Processing Fundamentals
8. CNN (G)
9. Minimax & Alpha-Beta Pruning (G)
10. Reinforcement Learning (Optional, after the exam for additional self-learning)

= Programming Assignments =
PA1 KNN Classifier for Sentiment Analysis
PA2 Retinal Vessel Segmentation using CNN
comment_grading:
= Grading scheme =
▷ Lab exercises 10% (5 graded labs, 2% each; 4 ungraded labs additionally)
▷ Programming assignments 30% (2 PAs, 15% each)
▷ Midterm exam 20% OR 0%
▷ Final exam 40% OR 60%
(The more beneficial one will be chosen for you automatically)

Desmond promised 25% A-range and ~46% B-range THIS semester. It is pretty competitive with many apostles coming for Desmond's teaching (seeing that the classroom of 126 capacity was packed on the first day and the quota had to be raised two times from 80 to 120).

Exams pretty much determine your grade, and you should **focus a lot on the concepts** instead of programming since ~75% of the exams will be short questions where you have to explain things. Often I feel stupid for not being able to answer those with total confidence, and since math equations and library functions (w/ documentation) will be provided, study even more on the concepts and rationale instead!

I got an A-, but considering my scores, I reckon I'm right on the edge of the A range.

An introductory course to A.I.

comment_teaching:
Most handsome, friendly, and supportive professor ever!!  Every engineering school student should experience Desmond's teaching at least once.

If you have questions, post on piazza (forum used for comp courses) or send Prof. Desmond an email!  The TA's and Professor are very willing to provide support and answer your questions.

Desmond also responds actively to chat box on zoom, lots of interactions with students during lessons.

Probably the hardest topic in this course is the first one, Python Fundamentals for AI.  In this topic, you will learn Numpy, and most importantly Numpy broadcasting.  Lots and lots of practice and experience is needed to master broadcasting, and whether or not you master it will make or break your grade.  Personally, I found it challenging because it's not related to programming, instead it really needs imagination.  You need to be able to visualize 2D or 3D data in your head and think about how you can reshape/ multiply them together to achieve your goal.  So the 2 semesters spent in 2011/2012 won't help you.  9 out of 100 marks from midterm are related to broadcasting, and whether you master it or not will determine if you pass many test cases for PA1.

comment_content:
An introductory course to A.I. I tend to divide the course into four parts:

----- Part Zero: Some soft stuff -----
-> History of AI (First part of the course)
-> AI ethics (Last part of the course)

----- Part One: Simple algorithm -----
1. Naive Bayes (Evaluate probability to predict class)
2. K-nearest (Predict class by majority voting)
3. K-means (Do grouping to a set of data)

----- Part Two: Artificial Neural Network -----
4. Multilayer perceptron
5. Image processing
6. Convolutional neural network

----- Part Three: Game Theory related -----
7. Minimax
8. Reinforcement learning (Optional)

Personal comment:

1. Before Naive Bayes, an introduction to Python is given (with many extra lessons!) Therefore there is no need to worry if you are not familiar with Python.
2. This is really an introductory course, in the sense that the requirement of math is even fewer than Andrew Ng's AI course in Coursera... But I find this course better anyway.
3. The part for reinforcement learning is actually a good starter to learn (discrete time) "stochastic process", which is an interesting topic in math. I personally find the related material in this course is more readable than that in MATH3425...
comment_teaching:
1. Enthusiasm: Desmond's enthusiasm in teaching makes the class lively. Because of that, this is the first course that I seldom feel bored or refused to attend the F2F class.

2. Extra material: Desmond is willing to distribute advanced detail to related topics through email. This is frequently done in first half of the course.
comment_workload:
Assignments: Only 5 labs and 2 programming assignments are required to submit. The labs are simple.

The only tricky assignment in this semester is PA1: It requires you to do modification to your code well to get full score, which strictly needs to apply vectorization everywhere instead of loops; PA2 is another extreme: You even do not have to think because the architecture is given - Because of that, this PA is even easier than regular lab...

Preparation for exam: Since necessary math formulas and codes commands are given in exam, the only thing which is essential to memorize is the rationale behind each algorithm. Indeed there are some other tricky things like broadcasting rule, do convolution or calculating number of trainable parameters and so on, but they can be understood once you are willing to practice.
comment_grading:
1. As usual, Desmond's course promises as large A range as possible.
2. Warning: This semester is more competitive than the last one! Given that the midterm exams in two semesters are about the same (in terms of style, difficulty...), last sem's mean is ~65, and this sem's mean is ~80...
3. However, the final exam in this semester is much harder. It requires deeper understanding (than last sem's final paper) to get high score, and there are more parts requiring you to hand-write codes.

Update: Fast grading! The final statistics is released in 3 days. Mean ~60 (confused, since Desmond says 59, but Canvas says 56), SD ~14, Highest score is 86. Comparing to the result in previous semester, you can see that this year's paper is apparently more challenging.

comment_content:
This is the first offering of this brand new course, the original content plan was the following:

1. Brief History of Artificial Intelligence
2. Naive Bayes
3. K-Nearest Neighbour
4. K-Means Clustering
5. Perceptron and Multi-Layer Perceptron
6. Fundamentals of Image Processing
7. Convolutional Neural Networks
8. Minimax and Alpha-beta Pruning
9. Reinforcement Learning (Value Iteration)*
10. Artificial Intelligence Ethics

*Later on, desmond realized that there is just not enough time to cover reinforcement learning and decided to leave that to summer.

As a brand new AI related course designed with a course code less than COMP4211 and COMP3211, I can see that desmond tried his best to maintain the balence ---- he tried to make the content decent but at the same time not too complicated to scare students away. So I should say the content is well designed and brings a good overview to different aspects of AI and ML.

I do think there are two possible improvements to the content:
1. I undestand things are meant to be kept simple so as to not scare students away, but there are some math that isn't that complicated but from my perspective should not be skipped. Such as for the backpropagation of MLP, the formula provided in the slides were just like things to simply memorize (although memorization was not required as the formula is provided in exams). But in fact that formula should only be applicable to sigmoid activation since even the derivative was pre-computed as a part of it. It's not even the general learning rule. The math behind this is simply chain rule, which is something understanable as long as we took MATH1013 I suppose(which is probably a course every single ust student takes?) So maybe it's better to explain about the deatailed math behind back propagation as it is essential for understanding gradient descent behind MLP.
2. The Numpy tutorial was really good. It is almost same as the python tutorial of CS231n. But I really also hoped to learn how to properly use pytorch or tensorflow when enrolling this course. Seldom do professional researchers use Keras in machine learning research, it's just too wrapped up and provides little flexibility. Pytorch and Tensorflow (not tensorflow keras) plays a greater role. I hope that the main library chosen for the PAs can be changed into pytorch or tensorflow in the future.
comment_teaching:
Desmond is always passionate about teaching. I can feel that he just literally loves to teach. He tries his best to make sure that every concept is explained clearly, and also engages actively with the chat  to answer questions. So very good teaching!

The only downside is that he always asks "Am I going too fast", when in fact, probably he knows it, that he is going too slow. Too much time are spent on telling jokes in Cantonese and Putonghua. And then additonal lectures need to be offered, and one topic had to be left till summer. I mentioned this in the review of another desmond's course and got a great bunch of down votes though. I don't know if everyone else find this amusing but for me sometimes it just really feels time consuming and just encourages me to watch recordings in 2x speed=(
comment_workload:
Workload can be divided into Labs, PAs, and exam revision.

10 labs, 5 out of which are graded. Each lab takes around 1-3 hours to finish depending on complexity.

2 PAs, both graded. PA1 was obviously more complicated and takes about one whole day (from morning to night, non-stop). But PA2 can be done in just 2 hours, it was even simpler than lab6 and lab8 which was probably the two most lengthy labs.

Expect one day each of full revision for the midterm and final exam. Pay attention to calculating image convolution and calculating number of parameter in CNN and MLP layers. (by one day, I also mean one whole day, from morning to night, non-stop)

Therefore, the overall workload is very small.
comment_grading:
Desmond is probably the most lenient grade giver in CSE.

He gives 25% A range in COMP1022p and COMP2012 (Since that is already the maximum A range allowed for non-H courses according to ust policies) (BAD GUY UST, allow for larger A range nah! :<) and gives an astonishing 60% A range in COMP2012H (because that's an H course and this is allowed). And Desmond also promised 25% Arange for this course as well.

So you can see that desmond is really pushing to the limit against the policies to give more As. Indeed a lenient grade giver.

Also I am so grateful that he provides 2 grading schemes to provide us a try and error chance in the midterm since there weren't any pastpaper available. It was so so so thoughtful of him to provide the second scheme so that we get a chance to get used to the exam question types for this course. One thing I learnt from this is don't give up even if you failed in the midterm. Cause if you prepare well for the final you can still do a great job. Thank you again desmond for the 2 schemes option!
Scheme 1: Lab(10%) + PA(30%) + Midterm(20%) + Final(40%)
Scheme 2: Lab(10%) + PA(30%) + Final(60%)

The higher one will be automatically chosen for you.

The distribution of both exams can either be found in Yogesh's review here: https://ust.space/review/COMP2211/view/o5SQis0xkdkDZzwGVlO8gqUHZ2mw28qX or in the course website under exam section here: https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2211/#exams

comment_content:
The topics cover a large range of AI techniques and applications, which may seem quite challenging but as they said in the course description, the content does not go too in-depth, giving you the gentle introduction you need to get excited about AI. Labs and PAs give you the chance to apply what you have learnt.

The course will help you begin communicating with others about AI, and also gives you an idea for what techniques are available to use for your own projects. It is a great intro course to anyone who is not too familiar with AI, but wants to learn what's available in the field.

You will need a good Python foundation to begin with, **familiarity with Numpy is preferred**. Lectures will include python code explanation, and the final exam will also involve writing your own code. Although the lectures reviewed Python at the beginning of the course, Desmond goes quite fast, basically like a COMP1021 crash course.

Keyword Syllabus
- History of AI
- Naive Bayes
- K-Nearest Neighbour
- K-Means Clustering
- Perceptron and Multi-Layer Perceptron
- Fundamentals of Image Processing
- Convolutional Neural Networks
- Minimax and Alpha-beta Pruning
- Reinforcement Learning (Value Iteration)
- Artificial Intelligence Ethics

Even though the lessons are in online-mode, his lectures are extremely lively, continuously asks the class questions and responding to questions in chat. He is very open to questions after lesson, even if it is out-of-syllabus. I can only imagine how much fun it would be when the lessons are held face-to-face.

comment_content:
Very rewarding, content/understanding-heavy AI crash course. Even if you come into this course knowing nothing about AI like myself, you will end up being able to understand most AI research, freely converse about it, and apply it in many areas of your future projects.

I would only recommend this course to anyone with a good background in programming and is willing to learn AI. That is, if your programming experience is only limited to COMP1021, you may find this course difficult, especially when you get to Numpy. I'd argue you cannot focus on the ideas of AI itself if you are hindered by the programming.

Python skills:
- Python basics
- Numpy
- Scikitlearn
- Keras
- Tensorflow

Content:
- Definition and history of AI
- Naive Bayes Classifier
- K-Nearest Neighbours
- K-Means
- Perceptron
- Multilayer Perceptron
- Convolutional Neural Network
- Minimax / Alpha-beta pruning
- (Not taught this semester) Reinforcement learning

The hard theory and maths behind the more complex stuff will not be tested, but supplementary slides were provided for more advanced students.
comment_teaching:
Desmond is very lively and explains concepts well. He repeats concepts to make sure you understand them. He's also open to questions after the lecture and also responds to emails timely.

I would recommend staying after class to participate in post-class discussions and Q&A's. You can also attend his Zoom office hours if you just want to chat with him, even if it may be off-topic.

The pace was not fully optimised this semester as this is the first offering. However, I am sure these issues will be ironed out in future semesters.
comment_workload:
- 9 Labs, 5 of which are graded
(There were 10 labs planned, but we ran out of time and couldn't do the reinforcement learning lab)
- 2 programming assignments

There may be supplementary lessons (sometimes at 8PM-10PM) that you are expected to attend.

The labs are a good way of learning and applying the ideas taught in class. They are submitted to ZINC, and your submissions are auto-graded every time you upload, unlike COMP1021.

The programming assignments take some time, and you *are* expected to develop your own test cases. They require a solid understanding of the material to complete. Also, as another review mentioned, try to get them uploaded to ZINC before the deadline, because the server tends to get overloaded near the due date.

PS. If you have a computer with a CUDA-supported GPU, I would recommend setting up Jupyter up on your local machine and doing the Labs/PAs there instead of Google Colab. It will make the code for the later labs/PAs run much faster, and of course you have no GPU quota unlike Colab. They even set up a 3080Ti for the grading of Lab 6 :P
comment_grading:
There are two different ways to assess your performance.

Laboratory exercises (10%) + Programming assignments (30%) + Midterm exam (20%) + Final exam (40%)

Desmond said he would try to give 25% A-range. Assuming the distribution remains normal, around 74% in the midterm indicates a good chance for A-range.

MIDTERM
--------------

The midterm is quite tricky. You will perform better in the exams if you play with the code that you've written in the Labs and PAs as well as example ones to gain a more intuitive understanding of AI.

It may just be because it's the first exam of this course, but time in the midterm was extremely tight. I finished with barely 5 minutes remaining, and had no time to check most of my answers. My friends had a similar experience with the timing.

FINAL
--------------

SD: 13.82
Median: 67
Max: 94.5

In the final, time is not as tight as the midterm. However, there are quite a few more conceptual questions which really test whether you understand the idea behind the concepts and whether you can extend them. It's also a bit more evil, in the sense that many questions depend on answers from previous parts.

Oh, I should also mention Desmond is one of the few profs who gives written feedback for every question on your exam, releases the markscheme/paper, and lets you appeal every question. This is becoming a rarity at UST and is very welcome.

OVERALL GRADE:
The overall mean is about 76% (don't have an exact figure) and should be about B range.

Seems that 87-88% overall can secure A-, 90 can secure A, and 92-93 can secure A+.
